summary: Jeff Atwood contrasts Google's enduring homepage simplicity with Yahoo's gradual feature bloat from 1996-2006, arguing that minimalism is a competitive advantage. He cites Google's obsessive word-counting discipline, 37signals' 'less is more' philosophy, and pushes back against Donald Norman's criticism that Google merely hides complexity. Atwood argues that users don't want to 'do anything else' beyond their core task, and that features ultimately don't matter to people who just want to complete a task and move on. The post champions relentless pursuit of simplicity as a design principle.
key_points: Google maintains homepage discipline through unusual feedback mechanisms like an anonymous user who emails word counts when they increase
37signals advocates 'one-downing' competitors rather than one-upping them with more features
Donald Norman's criticism that Google 'hides complexity' misses the point—users don't want the hidden features anyway
Feature bloat killed competitors like AltaVista while Google's restraint helped it dominate
Users don't want to learn, configure, or spend more than 10 seconds confused—they want task completion
money_quotes: It's like a scale that tells you that you've gained two pounds.
Instead of one-upping, try one-downing. Instead of outdoing, try underdoing.
Do less than your competitors to beat them.
Is Google simple? No. Google is deceptive. It hides all the complexity by simply showing one search box on the main page.
I don't want to do anything else. All I want is to find what I'm searching for.
They don't want to spend more than 10 consecutive seconds confused.
They just want simple, they want to get their task done and move on.
We should always be in pursuit of simplicity, in whatever form it takes.
key_insight: True product simplicity isn't about hiding complexity—it's about recognizing that users genuinely don't want the additional features you're tempted to build.
